choice2_text: Most CJ exercises sample a subset of the n(n-1)/2 possible paired comparisons among n objects such that each object is involved in the same or a similar number of comparisons (e.g. c=20). The resulting data is then analysed with a Bradley-Terry model. If instead we compared (n-20) of the objects with the SAME 20 objects we would create a data set where we could simply add up the number of wins and rank the objects on their score out of 20, without involving the Bradley Terry model at all. What are the theoretical and practical differences between these situations and which is preferable? Do the long scales with lots of decimal places created by a typical CJ analysis disguise the fact that you don't really have more information than you'd get from a 20-item test? choice2_text: Most CJ exercises sample a subset of the n(n-1)/2 possible paired comparisons among n objects such that each object is involved in the same or a similar number of comparisons (e.g. c=20). The resulting data is then analysed with a Bradley-Terry model. If instead we compared (n-20) of the objects with the SAME 20 objects we would create a data set where we could simply add up the number of wins and rank the objects on their score out of 20, without involving the Bradley Terry model at all. What are the theoretical and practical differences between these situations and which is preferable? Do the long scales with lots of decimal places created by a typical CJ analysis disguise the fact that you don't really have more information than you'd get from a 20-item test? choice2_text: Most CJ exercises sample a subset of the n(n-1)/2 possible paired comparisons among n objects such that each object is involved in the same or a similar number of comparisons (e.g. c=20). The resulting data is then analysed with a Bradley-Terry model. If instead we compared (n-20) of the objects with the SAME 20 objects we would create a data set where we could simply add up the number of wins and rank the objects on their score out of 20, without involving the Bradley Terry model at all. What are the theoretical and practical differences between these situations and which is preferable? Do the long scales with lots of decimal places created by a typical CJ analysis disguise the fact that you don't really have more information than you'd get from a 20-item test? How does Cronbach's Alpha from the 'fixed set' CJ design compare with the Scale Separation Reliability usually reported from the Bradley-Terry analysis, both in theory and in practice?

choice2_text: Most CJ exercises sample a subset of the n(n-1)/2 possible paired comparisons among n objects such that each object is involved in the same or a similar number of comparisons (e.g. c=20). The resulting data is then analysed with a Bradley-Terry model. If instead we compared (n-20) of the objects with the SAME 20 objects we would create a data set where we could simply add up the number of wins and rank the objects on their score out of 20, without involving the Bradley Terry model at all. What are the theoretical and practical differences between these situations and which is preferable? Do the long scales with lots of decimal places created by a typical CJ analysis disguise the fact that you don't really have more information than you'd get from a 20-item test? How does Cronbach's Alpha from the 'fixed set' CJ design compare with the Scale Separation Reliability usually reported from the Bradley-Terry analysis, both in theory and in practice?

choice2_text: Most CJ exercises sample a subset of the n(n-1)/2 possible paired comparisons among n objects such that each object is involved in the same or a similar number of comparisons (e.g. c=20). The resulting data is then analysed with a Bradley-Terry model. If instead we compared (n-20) of the objects with the SAME 20 objects we would create a data set where we could simply add up the number of wins and rank the objects on their score out of 20, without involving the Bradley Terry model at all. choice2_text: Most CJ exercises sample a subset of the n(n-1)/2 possible paired comparisons among n objects such that each object is involved in the same or a similar number of comparisons (e.g. c=20). The resulting data is then analysed with a Bradley-Terry model. If instead we compared (n-20) of the objects with the SAME 20 objects we would create a data set where we could simply add up the number of wins and rank the objects on their score out of 20, without involving the Bradley Terry model at all. What are the theoretical and practical differences between these situations and which is preferable? Do the long scales with lots of decimal places created by a typical CJ analysis disguise the fact that you don't really have more information than you'd get from a 20-item test? How does Cronbach's Alpha from the 'fixed set' CJ design compare with the Scale Separation Reliability usually reported from the Bradley-Terry analysis, both in theory and in practice?

choice2_text: Most CJ exercises sample a subset of the n(n-1)/2 possible paired comparisons among n objects such that each object is involved in the same or a similar number of comparisons (e.g. c=20). The resulting data is then analysed with a Bradley-Terry model. If instead we compared (n-20) of the objects with the SAME 20 objects we would create a data set where we could simply add up the number of wins and rank the objects on their score out of 20, without involving the Bradley Terry model at all. What are the theoretical and practical differences between these situations and which is preferable? Do the long scales with lots of decimal places created by a typical CJ analysis disguise the fact that you don't really have more information than you'd get from a 20-item test? choice2_text: Most CJ exercises sample a subset of the n(n-1)/2 possible paired comparisons among n objects such that each object is involved in the same or a similar number of comparisons (e.g. c=20). The resulting data is then analysed with a Bradley-Terry model. If instead we compared (n-20) of the objects with the SAME 20 objects we would create a data set where we could simply add up the number of wins and rank the objects on their score out of 20, without involving the Bradley Terry model at all. What are the theoretical and practical differences between these situations and which is preferable? Do the long scales with lots of decimal places created by a typical CJ analysis disguise the fact that you don't really have more information than you'd get from a 20-item test? How does Cronbach's Alpha from the 'fixed set' CJ design compare with the Scale Separation Reliability usually reported from the Bradley-Terry analysis, both in theory and in practice?

choice2_text: Most CJ exercises sample a subset of the n(n-1)/2 possible paired comparisons among n objects such that each object is involved in the same or a similar number of comparisons (e.g. c=20). The resulting data is then analysed with a Bradley-Terry model. If instead we compared (n-20) of the objects with the SAME 20 objects we would create a data set where we could simply add up the number of wins and rank the objects on their score out of 20, without involving the Bradley Terry model at all. choice2_text: Most CJ exercises sample a subset of the n(n-1)/2 possible paired comparisons among n objects such that each object is involved in the same or a similar number of comparisons (e.g. c=20). The resulting data is then analysed with a Bradley-Terry model. If instead we compared (n-20) of the objects with the SAME 20 objects we would create a data set where we could simply add up the number of wins and rank the objects on their score out of 20, without involving the Bradley Terry model at all. choice2_text: Most CJ exercises sample a subset of the n(n-1)/2 possible paired comparisons among n objects such that each object is involved in the same or a similar number of comparisons (e.g. c=20). The resulting data is then analysed with a Bradley-Terry model. If instead we compared (n-20) of the objects with the SAME 20 objects we would create a data set where we could simply add up the number of wins and rank the objects on their score out of 20, without involving the Bradley Terry model at all. What are the theoretical and practical differences between these situations and which is preferable? Do the long scales with lots of decimal places created by a typical CJ analysis disguise the fact that you don't really have more information than you'd get from a 20-item test? How does Cronbach's Alpha from the 'fixed set' CJ design compare with the Scale Separation Reliability usually reported from the Bradley-Terry analysis, both in theory and in practice?

If instead we compared (n-20) of the objects with the SAME 20 objects we would create a data set where we could simply add up the number of wins and rank the objects on their score out of 20, without involving the Bradley Terry model at all. What are the theoretical and practical differences between these situations and which is preferable? Do the long scales with lots of decimal places created by a typical CJ analysis disguise the fact that you don't really have more information than you'd get from a 20-item test? choice2_text: Most CJ exercises sample a subset of the n(n-1)/2 possible paired comparisons among n objects such that each object is involved in the same or a similar number of comparisons (e.g. c=20). The resulting data is then analysed with a Bradley-Terry model. If instead we compared (n-20) of the objects with the SAME 20 objects we would create a data set where we could simply add up the number of wins and rank the objects on their score out of 20, without involving the Bradley Terry model at all. What are the theoretical and practical differences between these situations and which is preferable? Do the long scales with lots of decimal places created by a typical CJ analysis disguise the fact that you don't really have more information than you'd get from a 20-item test? choice2_text: Most CJ exercises sample a subset of the n(n-1)/2 possible paired comparisons among n objects such that each object is involved in the same or a similar number of comparisons (e.g. c=20). The resulting data is then analysed with a Bradley-Terry model. If instead we compared (n-20) of the objects with the SAME 20 objects we would create a data set where we could simply add up the number of wins and rank the objects on their score out of 20, without involving the Bradley Terry model at all. What are the theoretical and practical differences between these situations and which is preferable? Do the long scales with lots of decimal places created by a typical CJ analysis disguise the fact that you don't really have more information than you'd get from a 20-item test? choice2_text: Most CJ exercises sample a subset of the n(n-1)/2 possible paired comparisons among n objects such that each object is involved in the same or a similar number of comparisons (e.g. c=20). The resulting data is then analysed with a Bradley-Terry model. If instead we compared (n-20) of the objects with the SAME 20 objects we would create a data set where we could simply add up the number of wins and rank the objects on their score out of 20, without involving the Bradley Terry model at all. What are the theoretical and practical differences between these situations and which is preferable? Do the long scales with lots of decimal places created by a typical CJ analysis disguise the fact that you don't really have more information than you'd get from a 20-item test? choice2_text: Most CJ exercises sample a subset of the n(n-1)/2 possible paired comparisons among n objects such that each object is involved in the same or a similar number of comparisons (e.g. c=20). The resulting data is then analysed with a Bradley-Terry model. If instead we compared (n-20) of the objects with the SAME 20 objects we would create a data set where we could simply add up the number of wins and rank the objects on their score out of 20, without involving the Bradley Terry model at all. What are the theoretical and practical differences between these situations and which is preferable? Do the long scales with lots of decimal places created by a typical CJ analysis disguise the fact that you don't really have more information than you'd get from a 20-item test? The resulting data is then analysed with a Bradley-Terry model. If instead we compared (n-20) of the objects with the SAME 20 objects we would create a data set where we could simply add up the number of wins and rank the objects on their score out of 20, without involving the Bradley Terry model at all. choice2_text: Most CJ exercises sample a subset of the n(n-1)/2 possible paired comparisons among n objects such that each object is involved in the same or a similar number of comparisons (e.g. c=20). The resulting data is then analysed with a Bradley-Terry model. If instead we compared (n-20) of the objects with the SAME 20 objects we would create a data set where we could simply add up the number of wins and rank the objects on their score out of 20, without involving the Bradley Terry model at all. choice2_text: Most CJ exercises sample a subset of the n(n-1)/2 possible paired comparisons among n objects such that each object is involved in the same or a similar number of comparisons (e.g. c=20). The resulting data is then analysed with a Bradley-Terry model. If instead we compared (n-20) of the objects with the SAME 20 objects we would create a data set where we could simply add up the number of wins and rank the objects on their score out of 20, without involving the Bradley Terry model at all. What are the theoretical and practical differences between these situations and which is preferable? Do the long scales with lots of decimal places created by a typical CJ analysis disguise the fact that you don't really have more information than you'd get from a 20-item test? choice2_text: Most CJ exercises sample a subset of the n(n-1)/2 possible paired comparisons among n objects such that each object is involved in the same or a similar number of comparisons (e.g. c=20). The resulting data is then analysed with a Bradley-Terry model. If instead we compared (n-20) of the objects with the SAME 20 objects we would create a data set where we could simply add up the number of wins and rank the objects on their score out of 20, without involving the Bradley Terry model at all. What are the theoretical and practical differences between these situations and which is preferable? Do the long scales with lots of decimal places created by a typical CJ analysis disguise the fact that you don't really have more information than you'd get from a 20-item test? How does Cronbach's Alpha from the 'fixed set' CJ design compare with the Scale Separation Reliability usually reported from the Bradley-Terry analysis, both in theory and in practice?

choice2_text: Most CJ exercises sample a subset of the n(n-1)/2 possible paired comparisons among n objects such that each object is involved in the same or a similar number of comparisons (e.g. c=20). The resulting data is then analysed with a Bradley-Terry model. If instead we compared (n-20) of the objects with the SAME 20 objects we would create a data set where we could simply add up the number of wins and rank the objects on their score out of 20, without involving the Bradley Terry model at all. What are the theoretical and practical differences between these situations and which is preferable? Do the long scales with lots of decimal places created by a typical CJ analysis disguise the fact that you don't really have more information than you'd get from a 20-item test? choice2_text: Most CJ exercises sample a subset of the n(n-1)/2 possible paired comparisons among n objects such that each object is involved in the same or a similar number of comparisons (e.g. c=20). The resulting data is then analysed with a Bradley-Terry model. If instead we compared (n-20) of the objects with the SAME 20 objects we would create a data set where we could simply add up the number of wins and rank the objects on their score out of 20, without involving the Bradley Terry model at all. What are the theoretical and practical differences between these situations and which is preferable? Do the long scales with lots of decimal places created by a typical CJ analysis disguise the fact that you don't really have more information than you'd get from a 20-item test? choice2_text: Most CJ exercises sample a subset of the n(n-1)/2 possible paired comparisons among n objects such that each object is involved in the same or a similar number of comparisons (e.g. c=20). choice2_text: Most CJ exercises sample a subset of the n(n-1)/2 possible paired comparisons among n objects such that each object is involved in the same or a similar number of comparisons (e.g. c=20). The resulting data is then analysed with a Bradley-Terry model. If instead we compared (n-20) of the objects with the SAME 20 objects we would create a data set where we could simply add up the number of wins and rank the objects on their score out of 20, without involving the Bradley Terry model at all. What are the theoretical and practical differences between these situations and which is preferable? Do the long scales with lots of decimal places created by a typical CJ analysis disguise the fact that you don't really have more information than you'd get from a 20-item test? How does Cronbach's Alpha from the 'fixed set' CJ design compare with the Scale Separation Reliability usually reported from the Bradley-Terry analysis, both in theory and in practice?

If instead we compared (n-20) of the objects with the SAME 20 objects we would create a data set where we could simply add up the number of wins and rank the objects on their score out of 20, without involving the Bradley Terry model at all. What are the theoretical and practical differences between these situations and which is preferable? Do the long scales with lots of decimal places created by a typical CJ analysis disguise the fact that you don't really have more information than you'd get from a 20-item test? How does Cronbach's Alpha from the 'fixed set' CJ design compare with the Scale Separation Reliability usually reported from the Bradley-Terry analysis, both in theory and in practice?

choice2_text: Most CJ exercises sample a subset of the n(n-1)/2 possible paired comparisons among n objects such that each object is involved in the same or a similar number of comparisons (e.g. c=20). The resulting data is then analysed with a Bradley-Terry model. If instead we compared (n-20) of the objects with the SAME 20 objects we would create a data set where we could simply add up the number of wins and rank the objects on their score out of 20, without involving the Bradley Terry model at all. What are the theoretical and practical differences between these situations and which is preferable? Do the long scales with lots of decimal places created by a typical CJ analysis disguise the fact that you don't really have more information than you'd get from a 20-item test? How does Cronbach's Alpha from the 'fixed set' CJ design compare with the Scale Separation Reliability usually reported from the Bradley-Terry analysis, both in theory and in practice?

choice2_text: Most CJ exercises sample a subset of the n(n-1)/2 possible paired comparisons among n objects such that each object is involved in the same or a similar number of comparisons (e.g. c=20). The resulting data is then analysed with a Bradley-Terry model. If instead we compared (n-20) of the objects with the SAME 20 objects we would create a data set where we could simply add up the number of wins and rank the objects on their score out of 20, without involving the Bradley Terry model at all. What are the theoretical and practical differences between these situations and which is preferable? Do the long scales with lots of decimal places created by a typical CJ analysis disguise the fact that you don't really have more information than you'd get from a 20-item test? choice2_text: Most CJ exercises sample a subset of the n(n-1)/2 possible paired comparisons among n objects such that each object is involved in the same or a similar number of comparisons (e.g. c=20). The resulting data is then analysed with a Bradley-Terry model. If instead we compared (n-20) of the objects with the SAME 20 objects we would create a data set where we could simply add up the number of wins and rank the objects on their score out of 20, without involving the Bradley Terry model at all. What are the theoretical and practical differences between these situations and which is preferable? Do the long scales with lots of decimal places created by a typical CJ analysis disguise the fact that you don't really have more information than you'd get from a 20-item test? How does Cronbach's Alpha from the 'fixed set' CJ design compare with the Scale Separation Reliability usually reported from the Bradley-Terry analysis, both in theory and in practice?

choice2_text: Most CJ exercises sample a subset of the n(n-1)/2 possible paired comparisons among n objects such that each object is involved in the same or a similar number of comparisons (e.g. c=20). The resulting data is then analysed with a Bradley-Terry model. If instead we compared (n-20) of the objects with the SAME 20 objects we would create a data set where we could simply add up the number of wins and rank the objects on their score out of 20, without involving the Bradley Terry model at all. What are the theoretical and practical differences between these situations and which is preferable? Do the long scales with lots of decimal places created by a typical CJ analysis disguise the fact that you don't really have more information than you'd get from a 20-item test? choice2_text: Most CJ exercises sample a subset of the n(n-1)/2 possible paired comparisons among n objects such that each object is involved in the same or a similar number of comparisons (e.g. c=20). The resulting data is then analysed with a Bradley-Terry model. If instead we compared (n-20) of the objects with the SAME 20 objects we would create a data set where we could simply add up the number of wins and rank the objects on their score out of 20, without involving the Bradley Terry model at all. What are the theoretical and practical differences between these situations and which is preferable? Do the long scales with lots of decimal places created by a typical CJ analysis disguise the fact that you don't really have more information than you'd get from a 20-item test? choice1_text: What cognitive strategies do judges use when making pairwise comparisons?
choice2_text: Most CJ exercises sample a subset of the n(n-1)/2 possible paired comparisons among n objects such that each object is involved in the same or a similar number of comparisons (e.g. c=20). The resulting data is then analysed with a Bradley-Terry model. If instead we compared (n-20) of the objects with the SAME 20 objects we would create a data set where we could simply add up the number of wins and rank the objects on their score out of 20, without involving the Bradley Terry model at all. What are the theoretical and practical differences between these situations and which is preferable? Do the long scales with lots of decimal places created by a typical CJ analysis disguise the fact that you don't really have more information than you'd get from a 20-item test? How does Cronbach's Alpha from the 'fixed set' CJ design compare with the Scale Separation Reliability usually reported from the Bradley-Terry analysis, both in theory and in practice?

choice2_text: Most CJ exercises sample a subset of the n(n-1)/2 possible paired comparisons among n objects such that each object is involved in the same or a similar number of comparisons (e.g. c=20). The resulting data is then analysed with a Bradley-Terry model. If instead we compared (n-20) of the objects with the SAME 20 objects we would create a data set where we could simply add up the number of wins and rank the objects on their score out of 20, without involving the Bradley Terry model at all. What are the theoretical and practical differences between these situations and which is preferable? Do the long scales with lots of decimal places created by a typical CJ analysis disguise the fact that you don't really have more information than you'd get from a 20-item test? choice1_text: Sliders: Rather than asking judges to make a binary choice, can we get better (more valid and reliable) results by asking judges to use a slider to indicate strength of preference?

choice1_text: Sliders: Rather than asking judges to make a binary choice, can we get better (more valid and reliable) results by asking judges to use a slider to indicate strength of preference?
choice2_text: Most CJ exercises sample a subset of the n(n-1)/2 possible paired comparisons among n objects such that each object is involved in the same or a similar number of comparisons (e.g. c=20). The resulting data is then analysed with a Bradley-Terry model. If instead we compared (n-20) of the objects with the SAME 20 objects we would create a data set where we could simply add up the number of wins and rank the objects on their score out of 20, without involving the Bradley Terry model at all. What are the theoretical and practical differences between these situations and which is preferable? Do the long scales with lots of decimal places created by a typical CJ analysis disguise the fact that you don't really have more information than you'd get from a 20-item test? How does Cronbach's Alpha from the 'fixed set' CJ design compare with the Scale Separation Reliability usually reported from the Bradley-Terry analysis, both in theory and in practice?

choice2_text: Most CJ exercises sample a subset of the n(n-1)/2 possible paired comparisons among n objects such that each object is involved in the same or a similar number of comparisons (e.g. c=20). The resulting data is then analysed with a Bradley-Terry model. If instead we compared (n-20) of the objects with the SAME 20 objects we would create a data set where we could simply add up the number of wins and rank the objects on their score out of 20, without involving the Bradley Terry model at all. What are the theoretical and practical differences between these situations and which is preferable? Do the long scales with lots of decimal places created by a typical CJ analysis disguise the fact that you don't really have more information than you'd get from a 20-item test? choice1_text: When does a one-dimensional ranking fail, and how should we model systematic intransitivity?
choice2_text: Most CJ exercises sample a subset of the n(n-1)/2 possible paired comparisons among n objects such that each object is involved in the same or a similar number of comparisons (e.g. c=20). The resulting data is then analysed with a Bradley-Terry model. If instead we compared (n-20) of the objects with the SAME 20 objects we would create a data set where we could simply add up the number of wins and rank the objects on their score out of 20, without involving the Bradley Terry model at all. What are the theoretical and practical differences between these situations and which is preferable? Do the long scales with lots of decimal places created by a typical CJ analysis disguise the fact that you don't really have more information than you'd get from a 20-item test? How does Cronbach's Alpha from the 'fixed set' CJ design compare with the Scale Separation Reliability usually reported from the Bradley-Terry analysis, both in theory and in practice?

choice1_text: How does the effectiveness of using CJ for assessment and learning vary by age?
choice2_text: Most CJ exercises sample a subset of the n(n-1)/2 possible paired comparisons among n objects such that each object is involved in the same or a similar number of comparisons (e.g. c=20). The resulting data is then analysed with a Bradley-Terry model. If instead we compared (n-20) of the objects with the SAME 20 objects we would create a data set where we could simply add up the number of wins and rank the objects on their score out of 20, without involving the Bradley Terry model at all. What are the theoretical and practical differences between these situations and which is preferable? Do the long scales with lots of decimal places created by a typical CJ analysis disguise the fact that you don't really have more information than you'd get from a 20-item test? How does Cronbach's Alpha from the 'fixed set' CJ design compare with the Scale Separation Reliability usually reported from the Bradley-Terry analysis, both in theory and in practice?

choice2_text: Most CJ exercises sample a subset of the n(n-1)/2 possible paired comparisons among n objects such that each object is involved in the same or a similar number of comparisons (e.g. c=20). The resulting data is then analysed with a Bradley-Terry model. If instead we compared (n-20) of the objects with the SAME 20 objects we would create a data set where we could simply add up the number of wins and rank the objects on their score out of 20, without involving the Bradley Terry model at all. What are the theoretical and practical differences between these situations and which is preferable? Do the long scales with lots of decimal places created by a typical CJ analysis disguise the fact that you don't really have more information than you'd get from a 20-item test? How does Cronbach's Alpha from the 'fixed set' CJ design compare with the Scale Separation Reliability usually reported from the Bradley-Terry analysis, both in theory and in practice?

If instead we compared (n-20) of the objects with the SAME 20 objects we would create a data set where we could simply add up the number of wins and rank the objects on their score out of 20, without involving the Bradley Terry model at all. What are the theoretical and practical differences between these situations and which is preferable? Do the long scales with lots of decimal places created by a typical CJ analysis disguise the fact that you don't really have more information than you'd get from a 20-item test? How does Cronbach's Alpha from the 'fixed set' CJ design compare with the Scale Separation Reliability usually reported from the Bradley-Terry analysis, both in theory and in practice?
choice2_text: How should we evaluate the accuracy/quality of individual paired comparisons, given that the Bradley-Terry model 'expects' unlikely outcomes? Can accuracy be distinguished from quality in this context? How best to establish the link between speed of judgement and accuracy/quality?
